questions.question_text: Under NCGS 93A-4.2, who is responsible for supervising Provisional Brokers in a brokerage office?
distractors: Any full Broker in the office
The firm's managing partner
The senior Provisional Broker
questions.explanation: NCGS 93A-4.2 specifically assigns supervisory responsibility for Provisional Brokers to the Broker-in-Charge (BIC). Full Brokers who are not designated as BIC do not have this supervisory authority, even if they are experienced.

questions.question_text: A BIC delegates daily trust account entries to an office administrator. The administrator makes an error causing a shortage. Who is responsible?
questions.correct_answer: The BIC remains responsible for the account
distractors: The office administrator who made the error
The firm owner exclusively
Responsibility is shared equally among all brokers
questions.explanation: Even when a BIC delegates trust account maintenance tasks to others, the BIC retains ultimate responsibility for the trust account. Delegation does not transfer accountability. The BIC must ensure proper procedures are followed and any errors are corrected.

questions.question_text: A BIC is the listing agent on a property. Her affiliated Provisional Broker brings a buyer for the same property. Can the BIC serve as designated dual agent?
questions.correct_answer: No, a BIC cannot be dual agent opposite her PB
distractors: Yes, with written consent from both parties
Yes, if the PB has over 2 years of experience
Only if another BIC supervises the transaction
questions.explanation: A BIC must not serve as a designated dual agent opposite an affiliated Provisional Broker. This restriction exists because the supervisory relationship between BIC and PB creates an inherent conflict that undermines the independence required for dual agency.

questions.question_text: A Provisional Broker closes a deal while her BIC is on vacation and unreachable. Is this a compliance issue?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, PBs cannot work without BIC supervision
distractors: No, if the PB has more than 1 year of experience
No, the BIC can review the file upon return
Only if the transaction exceeds $250,000
questions.explanation: Provisional Brokers must always work under the active supervision of their BIC. A BIC who is unreachable cannot provide the required oversight. The brokerage should designate a temporary BIC or ensure another qualified BIC covers during absences.

questions.question_text: A BIC resigns from a firm and no replacement is designated. What happens to the firm's Provisional Brokers?
distractors: They become full Brokers automatically
They may practice independently for 30 days
The firm owner can supervise them temporarily
questions.explanation: Without a designated BIC, Provisional Brokers cannot legally perform any brokerage activities. The firm must designate a new BIC before its PBs can resume practicing. A non-BIC firm owner or full Broker cannot substitute for a designated BIC in supervising PBs.

questions.question_text: A listing broker finds a ready, willing, and able buyer, but the seller refuses to close. Is the broker entitled to the commission?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the broker earned the fee
distractors: No, closing is required for commission
Only if the buyer sues for specific performance
Only half the agreed commission
questions.explanation: When a listing broker produces a ready, willing, and able buyer under the listing terms, the broker has earned the commission regardless of whether the seller follows through. The seller's refusal to close does not negate the broker's right to compensation.

questions.question_text: A listing agent discovers that the seller's spouse refuses to sign the deed at closing. Does the broker still earn the fee?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, the broker is still entitled to the fee
distractors: No, the transaction must close for payment
Only if the listing agreement addresses this
No, a spousal refusal voids the commission
questions.explanation: A broker who has procured a ready, willing, and able buyer is entitled to the fee even if the seller's spouse refuses to sign. The broker fulfilled the listing obligation by producing a qualified buyer. The seller's inability to deliver clear title does not extinguish the broker's earned commission.

questions.question_text: A buyer agent holds an active license and a written buyer agency agreement. What additional element must exist for the agent to earn compensation?
questions.correct_answer: The agent must procure the property the buyer buys
distractors: The agent must also hold a BIC designation
The transaction must close within 60 days
The seller must agree to pay the buyer agent
questions.explanation: For a buyer agent to earn compensation, three elements must exist: an active license, a written buyer agency agreement, and the agent must have procured the property the buyer ultimately purchases. Seller agreement to pay is not required.

questions.question_text: A seller and buyer secretly agree to cancel a contract to avoid paying the listing broker's commission. What is the broker's right?
questions.correct_answer: The broker is still entitled to the commission
distractors: The broker loses the right to any fee
The broker can only recover actual expenses
The broker must renegotiate with the seller
questions.explanation: When a buyer and seller conspire to cancel a contract to avoid paying a commission, the listing broker who procured the ready, willing, and able buyer remains entitled to the full commission. The parties cannot collude to deprive the broker of earned compensation.

questions.question_text: A broker lets her license lapse before closing a transaction she negotiated. Can she collect her commission?
questions.correct_answer: No, the license must be active when services are rendered
distractors: Yes, if the listing was signed while she was active
Yes, the commission was earned at contract
Only if she reinstates within 30 days of closing
questions.explanation: A broker must hold an active license at the time brokerage services are performed. If the license lapses before the transaction closes, the broker cannot legally collect compensation. Maintaining active status through closing is essential to preserving commission rights.

questions.question_text: How must a Provisional Broker receive her commission from a transaction?
distractors: Directly from the closing attorney
From the seller at closing
From the co-operating brokerage firm
questions.explanation: In North Carolina, Provisional Brokers must receive all compensation through their supervising BIC. They cannot be paid directly by clients, closing attorneys, or cooperating brokerages. This rule ensures proper oversight of financial transactions involving PBs.

questions.question_text: An out-of-state broker wants to co-broke on a North Carolina listing. She holds an active license in her home state. What restriction applies?
questions.correct_answer: She must not enter NC for brokerage activity
distractors: She must obtain a full NC broker license
She can operate freely with her home license
She needs written NCREC approval first
questions.explanation: A foreign broker with an active home-state license may co-broke with a NC broker but must not physically enter North Carolina to perform brokerage activities. The out-of-state broker refers the client and shares the fee but conducts no NC-based brokerage work.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a valid fee arrangement for a buyer agent in North Carolina?
questions.correct_answer: A retainer fee paid at the start of services
distractors: A percentage of the buyer's mortgage amount
An hourly rate deducted from property taxes
A flat fee paid by the appraiser
questions.explanation: Valid fee types for buyer agents include retainers, success fees, and sharing the listing broker's offered fee. A retainer paid at engagement is a legitimate arrangement. Fees based on mortgage amounts or paid by appraisers are not standard compensation structures.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent wants to include her compensation request in the purchase offer submitted to the seller. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, buyer-agent compensation may be in offers
distractors: No, compensation must be separate from offers
Only if the listing broker agrees in advance
Only for transactions over $300,000
questions.explanation: Under current NC rules, buyer-agent compensation may now be included in purchase offers presented to sellers. This change reflects evolving industry practices around buyer representation and compensation transparency.

questions.question_text: A full Broker affiliated with a firm closes a sale. Who pays her commission?
questions.correct_answer: The BIC of the firm she is affiliated with
distractors: The buyer directly at closing
The listing broker of the other firm
The closing attorney from escrow funds
questions.explanation: Even full Brokers affiliated with a firm receive compensation through the firm's BIC, not directly from clients or cooperating brokerages. This maintains proper accounting and ensures all firm transactions flow through the designated BIC.
